questions.prompt: Look at those dark clouds! Complete: "It ___ rain this afternoon."
questions.explanation: When we see evidence now (dark clouds), we predict with "be going to": it is going to rain ✓. "Are going to" does not agree with "it", "going to" drops the verb be, and "goes to" is the present simple, not a prediction.

questions.prompt: Complete: "A taxi is ___ than the Underground in London."
questions.explanation: "Expensive" is a long adjective, so its comparative is "more expensive than" ✓. "Expensiver" and "more expensiver" are impossible forms, and "most expensive" is the superlative, not a comparison of two things.

questions.prompt: Complete: "That was ___ day of the whole trip — everyone laughed a lot."
questions.explanation: "Happy" ends in -y, so the superlative changes y → i and adds -est, with "the": the happiest ✓. "The happyest" keeps the y wrongly, "the most happy" is wrong for a short -y adjective, and "happiest" forgets "the".

questions.prompt: Complete these directions: "Go past the bank, ___ the second turning on your left, and the café is opposite the park."
questions.explanation: Directions use the base verb: take the second turning ✓. "Taking", "to take" and "takes" are not used to give an instruction — you simply tell the person to "take" the turning.

questions.prompt: Complete the question: "___ they going to see a play at the theatre tonight?"
questions.explanation: A "be going to" question with "they" begins with "Are": Are they going to see…? ✓ "Do" and "Does" belong to the present simple, and "Is" does not agree with the plural "they".

questions.prompt: It is raining. Which suggestion is written correctly?

options.text: We could to visit an indoor market instead.
We could visit an indoor market instead.
We coulding visit an indoor market instead.
We could visiting an indoor market instead.

questions.explanation: "We could" is followed by the base verb: We could visit ✓. "We could to visit" adds an extra "to", "we coulding" invents an impossible form, and "we could visiting" uses -ing where the base verb is needed.
